Facts.text: Flavius Constantine becomes Co-Emperor as Emperor Constantius III of the Western Roman Empire with Honorius
Smoke Imix [Smoke Jaguar] becomes the 12th ruler of the Maya city of Copán - will rule for 67 years
Heavy storm strikes Dutch coast, killing large numbers
Vatican convicts scholar and wayward friar Giordano Bruno of heresy and turn him over civil authorities upon his refusal to recant to mete out his punishment of death
Robert Devereux, 2nd Earl of Essex, leads an unsuccessful revolt in London against Queen Elizabeth
Gunpowder is used in a mining operation instead of mechanical tools in present-day Slovakia, reportedly the first time explosives had been used in mining
Sobornoye Ulozheniye of 1649: Russia adopts new code of laws, consolidating serfdom (OS 29 Jan)
French and Native American troops attack and destroy Schenectady settlement in New York Province, either killing or capturing its inhabitants, revenge for the Lachine massacre
Lord Halifax resigns as Lord Privy Seal
William & Mary College is chartered in Williamsburg in the Dominion and Colony of Virginia (second in North America)
The Supreme Privy Council is established in Russia
First opera performance in North American colonies, "Flora," opens in Charleston, South Carolina
Comet C/1743 C1 approaches within 0.0390 AUs of Earth
French and Spanish fleet leave Toulon
Minor earthquake in London
Simon Willard patents banjo clock
Franz I of Austria declares war on France
1st US Vice-President chosen by Senate, Richard Johnson (Van Buren admin)
New Roman Republic established in Italy
The Devil's Footprints, hoof-like marks mysteriously appear for over 60km after a snowfall in southern Devon, England
The Smithsonian Institution Building 'The Castle' first opens to the public on the Mall in Washington D.C.
Confederate States of America organizes in Montgomery, Alabama (US Civil War)
Battle of Roanoke Island NC, Federals gain control of Pamlico Sound
Martin Robison Delany, 1st black major in US Army appointed during US Civil War
The Ausgleich (compromise) establishes the Dual Monarchy of Austria-Hungary
Sandford Fleming first proposes dividing the world into 24 equal time zones and adopting a Universal Standard Time at a meeting of the Royal Canadian Institute (adopted 1884)
Louis Waterman begins experiment to invent the fountain pen
Aurora Ski Club of Red Wing, Minnesota becomes the 1st US ski club

The Dawes Act authorizes the President of the United States to survey Native American tribal land and divide it into individual allotments. Those who accepted allotments and lived separately from the tribe were granted United States citizenship.
Flood ravages Dutch coast
US Enforcement Act repealed, making it easier to disenfranchise blacks
Georges Feydeau's farce "Le Dindon" premieres in Paris
Western Conference forms to be first Western college football season, later renamed Big 10 Conference
John Ames Sherman patents 1st envelope folding & gumming machine in Massachusetts
Cyclone hits Tahiti & adjacent islands, killing some 10,000 people
Wilhelmina '08 soccer team forms in Weert, Netherlands
France & Germany sign treaty about Morocco
The Boy Scouts of America is incorporated by William D. Boyce
US helps overthrow President Miguel Devila of Honduras
1st eastbound US transcontinental flight lands in Jacksonville, Florida
British Emissary journeys to Berlin to suggest that Britain might support German colonial aspirations in Africa if Germany agrees to hold her current naval strength
General Zamon becomes President of Haiti
French cruiser "Admiral Charner" torpedoed off Syrian coast, kills 374
NL votes down Charlie Ebbets proposal to limit 25 cent seats
"Stars & Stripes,", weekly US armed forces newspaper, 1st published
Bolshevik troops capture Odessa, bringing an end to foreign involvement in the resistance against Bolshevik rule
Radio arrives at the White House
Coal mine explosion at Dawson, New Mexico kills 120
German NSDAP Volkischer Beobachter newspaper becomes a daily
1st US coast-to-coast radio hookup: General John Joseph Carty speech in Chicago
German Reichstag decides to apply for League of Nations membership
Sean O'Casey's "Plough & Stars" opens at Abbey Theater Dublin
Belgian-Swiss treaty signed
1st transatlantic TV image received at Hartsdale, NY
KOY-AM in Phoenix Arizona begins radio transmissions
"Happy Days Are Here Again" by Benny Mereoff hits #1
Gas explosion Fire in Fushun-coal mine, Manchuria kills 3,000
American speed skater Irving Jaffee wins the 10,000m at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics; clinches distance double after also taking out the 5,000m
-23°F (-31°C), Seminole, Texas (state record)
1st flight of all-metal Boeing 247
Export-Import Bank organizes in Washington, D.C.
Gaston Doumergue forms new French government
1st ski jumping tournament at Red Wing, Minnesota
First-ever NFL Draft: Jay Berwanger from the University of Chicago is first pick by Philadelphia Eagles
German alpine skier Christi Cranz wins the inaugural Olympic women’s combined gold medal at the Garmisch-Partenkirchen Winter Games ahead of teammate Käthe Grasegger
Lewis & Hamilton's musical "Two for the Show" premieres in NYC
Lodtz, 1st large ghetto established by Nazis in Poland
Radio presenter and National Socialist Max Blokzijl begins Nazi propaganda on Dutch national radio (executed for it in 1946)
Japanese armored barges cross Strait of Johore to attack Singapore (WWII)
Red Army recaptures Kursk on the eastern front
1st African American reporter accredited to White House, Harry McAlpin
German submarine U-762 sunk off Ireland by British forces with the loss of all 50 crewmen
Allied air attack on Goch, Kleef, Kalkar and Reichswald
Jan van der Hoorn wins 8th Dutch 11-cities skating race (10:51)
KSD (now KSDK) TV channel 5 in St Louis, MO (NBC) begins broadcasting
Canada beats Switzerland 3-0, to regain the Olympic ice hockey title at the St. Moritz Winter Games; forward Walter Halder top scores with 29 points in Canada’s 5th Olympic title
V Winter Olympic Games close at St Moritz, Switzerland
Hungarian cardinal József Mindszenty sentenced to life in prison
"RCA Victor Show Starring Dennis Day" debuts on NBC TV
WLVA (now WSET) TV channel 13 in Lynchburg-Roanoke, VA (ABC) begins
The Government of Sindh abolished Jagirdari system in the province. One million acres (4000 km²) of land thus acquired is to be distributed among the landless peasants.
Mine disaster in Quaregnon Belgium, 8 die
Edgar Whitehead succeeds Garfield Todd as premier of South Rhodesia
French planes bomb Sakiet, Tunisia, 75 die
KIRO TV channel 7 in Seattle, WA (CBS) begins broadcasting
US Congress opens hearings looking into payola
In Paris, 8 people killed at a protest against independence for the French colony Algeria
KACB TV channel 3 in San Angelo, TX (NBC) begins broadcasting
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
AFL's Dallas Texans become KC Chiefs
First transmission of Clandestine Voice of Iraqi People (Communist)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
US Rep Martha Griffiths address gets civil rights protection for women being added to the 1964 Civil Rights Act
Eastern DC-7B crashes into Atlantic off Jones Beach, NJ, kills 84
President Johnson deploys 1st US combat troops to South Vietnam, with 3500 marines sent to protect key US airbase near Da Nang
English Pirate Radio UKGM closes down
Longest losing streak in Toronto Maple Leaf history (10 games)
Peter (Asher) & Gordon (Waller) discontinue their singing partnership
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Film "Planet of the Apes", based on Pierre Boulle's 1963 novel "La Planète des singes" premieres in New York City
Orangeburg Massacre: highway patrol officers kill 3 students and injure 27 others demonstrating at South Carolina State University, 1st student killing by law enforcement in the US
Last edition of American magazine "Saturday Evening Post"
Meteorite weighing over 1 ton falls in Chihuahua, Mexico
NASDAQ Composite stock market index debuts with 50 companies and a starting value of 100
Operation Lam Son 719 begins, a limited South Vietnamese incursion into Laos
Jean Kerrs "Finishing Touches" premieres in NYC
Mushtaq & Asif Iqbal make 350 stand for Pakistan's 4th wicket v NZ
Senate names seven members to investigate Watergate scandal
American television soap opera "The Secret Storm" ends a 20 year run on CBS
Three US astronauts return to Earth after a 85 days in the US space station, Skylab
TV sitcom "Good Times", spin-off from "Maude", starring Esther Rolle, John Amos, and J.J. Walker, premieres on CBS TV
1800 Unification church couples wed at mass ceremony in Korea
NHL Washiongton Capitols only got one shot in a period againstNew York Islanders
Largest crowd at Cleveland Coliseum (Cavs vs Wash-21,130)
Earthquake in San Francisco, at 5.0, strongest since 1966
Crown Prince Sad Abdallah al-Salim Al Sabah becomes Prime Minister of Kuwait
Proceedings of the US Senate are broadcast on radio for the first time
Denis Sassou-Nguesso becomes the President of the Republic of the Congo for the first time
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Los Angeles Dodgers trade 2B Davey Lopes to Oakland A's, breaking up MLB's longest-playing infield (Garvey-Lopes-Russell-Cey) after 8 1/2 seasons
Eric Peters sets transatlantic sailboat record (E-W)-46 days
Prize stallion and Derby winner Shergar kidnapped in Ireland, never to be found causing Lloyd's of London to payout $10.6 million insurance
Tina Howe's play "Painting Churches" premieres in NYC
For the 1st time eight people are in space
Soyuz T-10 launches with crew of 3 to Salyut 7
XIV Winter Olympic Games open in Sarajevo, Yugoslavia
Bruce Morris, Marshall Univ, makes a 92' 5½" basketball shot
Michael Gross swims world record 800m freestyle (7:38.75)
1984 Summer Olympics LAPD bomb squad chief Arleigh McCree, and his partner Officer Ronald Ball of the Firearms and explosives unit are killed while trying to dismantle two pipe bombs
5'7" Spud Webb of the Atlanta Hawks wins the NBA Slam Dunk Contest
NASA launches DOD-2
American jockeyChris Antleybegins record 64-day consecutive winning streak; ends 1 May and includes 147 winners from 486 mounts
Five centimeters of snow falls in the outskirts of Los Angeles
US Boeing 707 crashes into Santa Maria mountain, 145 die
"60 Minutes" commentator Andy Rooney suspended by CBS for racial remarks attributed to him by a gay magazine
David Hares play "Racing Demon" premieres in London at the National Theatre
"I'm Too Sexy" by Right Said Fred peaks at #1
Ulysses spacecraft passes Jupiter
XVI Winter Olympic Games opens in Albertville, France
GM sues NBC, alleging that "Dateline NBC" program had rigged 2 car-truck crashes to show that 1973-87 GM pickups were prone to fires
Sukhoi-24 crashes into a Tupolev passenger flight, killing 134 people
"Sátántangó", Hungarian film directed by Béla Tarr, starring Mihály Víg, Putyi Horváth, is released
6.4 earthquake at Trujillo, Colombia (46+ killed)
The massive Internet collaboration "24 Hours in Cyberspace" takes place
US Congress passes the Communications Decency Act
Finland beats Sweden, 6-0 at the Aqua Wing Arena in Nagano, Japan in the first female ice hockey game played in Olympic history
NHL stops season stops to accommodate the Nagano Winter Olympics; returns February 24
XIX Winter Olympic Games open in Salt Lake City, Utah, United States
Leaders of both Palestine and Israel declare a truce in what many hope will be a "new era of peace"
Nebraska bans electric chair as sole execution method
747-8 Freighter takes off for its maiden flight from Paine Field, Washington
100, 000 people march to demand justice for the atrocities of the Bangladesh Liberation War in Dhaka
16 people are killed and 27 are wounded by a market bombing in Kalaya, Pakistan
29 people are killed and 69 are injured in a series of Iraq bombings
A massive blizzard begins in the US and Canada that resulted in 15 deaths, 5,300 cancelled flights, and loss of power for 900,000 people
15 people are killed & 130 injured in a hotel fire in Medina, Saudi Arabia
University of Leiden established byWilliam I of Orange, given the motto "Praesidium Libertatis" (oldest university in Netherlands)
King James Idisbands the English parliament
Isaac Newtonreads his first optics paper before the Royal Society in London
Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's play "Stella" premieres in Hamburg
Battle of Eylau ends inconclusively betweenNapoleon's forces and the Russian Empire, first battle Napoleon isn't victorious
"The Birth of a Nation" the first 12-reel film in America, directed byD. W. Griffith, starring Lillian Gish and Mae Marsh, premieres at Clune's Auditorium in Los Angeles
Marcus Garveyenters federal prison in Atlanta
Disney Brothers Cartoon Studio becomesWalt DisneyStudios
PanditJawaharlal Nehrusucceeds Mahatma Gandhi as chairman of India Congress Party
Maxwell Anderson's dramatic play "Masque of Kings" premieres in NYC
Congress advisesFDRthat, Americans of Japanese descent should be locked up en masse so they wouldn't oppose the US war effort
Igor Stravinsky's orchestral piece "Danses Concertantes" premieres in Los Angeles, California
Nazi architectAlbert Speerappointed Minister of Armaments byAdolf Hitlerafter death of Fritz Todt in a plane crash
Paul Brownagrees to coach the new American football expansion team in Cleveland, which would later be named the Cleveland Browns after their coach
Béla Bartók's 3rd Piano Concerto premieres in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, by the Philadelphia Orchestra conducted byEugene Ormandywith György Sándor as piano soloist
Portuguese DictatorAntónio de Oliveira Salazarforbids opposition parties
Georgy Malenkovresigns as USSR premier and is replaced by Nikolai Bulganin
American "Stardust" composer and character actorHoagy Carmichaelreceives a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame
Boston CelticBill Russellbecomes 1st NBAer with 50 rebounds (51)

Queen Elizabeth IIof the United Kingdom issues an Order-in-Council stating that she and her family will be known as the House of Windsor and that her descendants will take the name "Mountbatten-Windsor"
Peter Shaffer's musical "Royal Hunt of the Sun" premieres in London
Motown Records releaseThe Supremes' single "Stop In the Name of Love"
Royal Albert Hall cancels scheduled concert featuringFrank Zappa, due to concerns about indecent lyrics
Josh Gibsonand Buck Leonard elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
Ringo Starrreleases a cover of "You're Sixteen" as a single in UK; originally released by Johnny Burnette in 1960
Pete Maravichscores 47 points as the New Orleans Jazz end a 28-game NBA road losing streak with a 106-102 win over the Hawks in Atlanta
"Taxi Driver" directed byMartin Scorsese, starringRobert De NiroandJodie Foster, premieres in NYC (Palme d'Or 1976)
Hustler publisherLarry Flyntsentenced on obscenity charges
Ariel Sharonresigns from Israeli government after an inquiry shows he was indirectly responsible for the killings of hundreds of people in 1982
Baseball ordersMickey Mantleto sever ties with Claridge Casino
Opposition leaderKim Dae-jungreturns to South Korea
Jon Woo's action film "A Better Tomorrow", starring Chow Yun-fat, premieres in Hong Kong
Roger Clemenssigns record $5,380,250 per year Red Sox contract
Jack Nicholsonuses a golf club to attack a car
Kapil Devsets world record for Test Cricket wickets with 432
Motley Crue drummerTommy Leeis charged with possession of a loaded firearm
NFL & Cleveland allowsArt Modellto move his NFL franchise to Baltimore but he had to leave the Browns' name behind

Reprise Records releases "Both Sides Now", Canadian singer-songwriterJoni Mitchell's 17th studio album, a collection of torch songs, including re-worked versions of two of her own, backed by an orchestra
46th Grammy Awards:Coldplay's "Clocks", and Evanescence win
48th Grammy Awards: Boulevard Of Broken Dreams,John Legendwin
51st Grammy Awards: Best Album "Raising Sand" and Record of the Year "Please Read The Letter", both byRobert Plant&Alison Krausswin
Al Gore's book "An Inconvenient Truth" wins a Grammy Award for Best Spoken Word Album
SingerRihannacancels performance at the Grammys after being assaulted by boyfriendChris Brown
Mary, Queen of Scots, is beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle at age 44 after being convicted of plotting to assassinate Elizabeth I in the Babington Plot
Yaroslav II Vsevolodovich, Grand Prince of Vladimir (1238-46), restored Russia after Mongol invasion, birthplace unknown (d. 1246)
Afonso IV, King of Portugal (1325-57) called the Brave, born in Lisbon, Kingdom of Portugal (d. 1357)
Ulrich, Duke of Württemberg, born in Riquewihr, Alsace (d. 1550)
Théodore-Agrippa d'Aubigné, French soldier, historian and poet (Histoire Universelle / Universal History), born in Pons, France (d. 1630)[1][2]
Robert Burton, British writer and Anglican clergyman (The Anatomy of Melancholy), born in Lindley, Leicestershire, England (d. 1640)
Jacob Praetorius, German Baroque organist, composer, and pedagogue, born in Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1651)
Samuel Butler, English poet and satirist (Hudibras), born in Strensham, England (d. 1680) [date baptized]
Gabriel Daniel, French Jesuit, historian and historiographer of France, appointed by King Louis XIV (History of France), born in Rouen, France (d. 1728)[1]
Charles-Jean-François Hénault, French writer and historian (A Chronological Abridgement of the History of France), born in Paris, Kingdom of France (d. 1770)[1]
Emanuel Swedenborg, Swedish philosopher, mathematician, and mystic (The New Church)), born in Stockholm, Swedish Empire (d. 1772)  [O.S. date Jan. 29]
Daniel Bernoulli, Dutch-Swiss mathematician, and physicist (10 time French Award), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1782)
Vaclav Jan Kopriva, Czech composer and organist, born in Cítoliby, Kingdom of Bohemia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1789)
Sakuramachi [Teruhito], 115th Emperor of Japan (1735-47), born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1750)
André Grétry, French opera composer, born in Prince-Bishopric of Liège (d. 1813)
Louis d'Affry, French general, military governor of Switzerland and mayor of Freiburg, born in Freiburg im Üechtland, Switzerland (d. 1810)
Joseph Leopold von Eybler, Austrian composer, born in Schwechat, Austrian Empire (d. 1846)
Ludwig Wilhelm Maurer, German composer and pianist, born in Potsdam, Margraviate of Brandenburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1878)
Caroline Augusta of Bavaria, Empress of Austria and Queen of consort of Hungary, Croatia and Slavonia, born in Mannheim, Electoral Palatinate (d. 1873)
Friedlieb Ferdinand Runge, German chemist (Chinoline), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1867)
Moritz Gottlieb Saphir, Austrian satirical writer and journalist, born in Lovasbereny, near Budapest (d. 1858)
Richard Lemon Lander, British explorer, born in Truro, Cornwall (d. 1834)
Benjamin Waterhouse Hawkins, English sculptor and naturalist, born in Bloomsbury, London (d. 1894)
Norbert Burgmüller, German composer, born in Düsseldorf, Grand Duchy of Berg (d. 1836)
Edwin Denison Morgan, American politician, Governor of New York, born in Washington, Massachusetts (d. 1883)
Jacob Georg Agarah, Swedish botanist, born in Lund, Sweden (d. 1901)
Richard S. Ewell, US Army officer and Confederate General (Battle of Gettysburg, Battle of Spotsylvania Court House), born in Georgetown, Washington D.C. (d. 1872)
Austin Blair, American politician, Governor of Michigan, born in New York (d. 1894)
John Ruskin, English writer and art critic who championed Gothic Revivalist and the Pre-Raphaelites, born in London, England (d. 1900)
Maxime Du Camp, French writer, traveler and early photographer (Les Buveurs de Cendres), born in Paris, France (d. 1894)
Barnard Elliott Bee, American military officer (US Army, 1845-61; Confederate Army, 1861), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1861)
Antonio Cagnoni, Italian composer, born in Godiasco, Kingdom of Lombardy–Venetia (d. 1896)
Hugh Price Hughes, Welsh social reformer, born in Carmarthen, Wales (d. 1902)
Kate O'Flaherty Chopin, American writer, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1904)
Theodor Lessing, German writer, born in Hanover, Germany (d. 1933)
Paula Modersohn-Becker, German Expressionist painter (1st known woman to paint nude self-portraits), born in Dresden-Friedrichstadt (d. 1907)
Helen Homans, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1906), born in Englewood, New Jersey (d. 1949)
Leonard Moon, English cricket batsman (4 Tests, Cambridge University CC, Middlesex CCC), born in London, England (d. 1916)
Martin Buber, Austrian-Israeli dialogue philosopher and theologist (Ich und Du), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1965)
Franz Marc, German Expressionist painter (co-founder of journal Blaue Reiter), born in Munich, Germany (d. 1916)

(William) Crickett Smith, American ragtime, and jazz cornetist and trumpeter (James Reese Europe's Clef Club; Ziegfeld's Midnight Frolics; Louis Mitchell's Jazz Kings), and bandleader, born in Emporia, Kansas (d. 1944)
George Siegmann, American actor (Birth of Nation, Queen of Sheba, Oliver Twist), born in New York City (d. 1928)
Thomas Selfridge, American army officer, 1st person to die in an airplane crash, born in San Francisco, California (d. 1908)
Joseph Schumpeter, Austria-American economist and minister of finance, born in Triesch, Habsburg Moravia (d. 1950)
Reginald "Snowy" Baker, Australian boxer (Olympic silver middleweight 1908), rugby union halfback (2 caps) and actor (The Sword of Valor), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1953)
Charlie Ruggles, American actor (Bringing Up Baby, The Patchwork Girl of Oz), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1970)
Edith Evans, British actress (Tom Jones, David Copperfield), born in London, England (d. 1976)
Matthijs Vermeulen, Dutch composer and music journalist, born in Helmond, Netherlands (d. 1967)
Claro Mayo Recto, Filipino politician, nationalist (opponent of US colonialism) and poet, born in Tiaong, Philippines (d. 1960)

Fritz Todt, German construction engineer, Reich Minister for Armaments and Munitions Reichs minister and Head of the Organization Todt, born in Pforzheim, Grand Duchy of Baden, German Empire (d. 1942)
Ba Maw, Burmese politician, lawyer, and first Burma Premier (1937-9), born in Maubin, British India (d. 1977)
King Vidor, American film director (War & Peace, Stella Dallas), born in Galveston, Texas (d. 1982)
Ludwig Marcuse, German philosopher (Obscene: The history of an indignation), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1971)
Orville Caldwell, American actor (Patsy, French Doll, Last Warning), born in Oakland, California (d. 1967)
Zakir Husain, 3rd President of India (1967-69) and scholar, born in Hyderabad, Hyderabad State (d. 1969)
Andrés Soriano, Spanish Filipino industrialist (San Miguel Brewery, Philippine Airlines), born in Manila, Spanish Philippines (d. 1964)
(Alonzo) "Lonnie" Johnson, American blues and jazz guitarist, electric violinist, singer and songwriter, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1970)
Guy Warrack, Scottish composer, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1986)
John Cameron, Scottish judge, born in London (d. 1996)
Janina Spychajowa-Kurkowska, Polish archer (5x record world champion), born in Starosielce, Russian Empire (d. 1979)
Demchugdongrub, Mongolian politician and head of state of Mengjiang (1939-45), born in Sonid Right Banner, China (d. 1966)[1]
Lyle Talbot [Lysle Henderson], American actor (The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1996)
Greta Keller, Austrian-born cabaret singer and actress, born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1977)
Igor' Fyodorovich Belza [Boelza], Soviet composer, born in Kielce, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1994)
Edgar Palm, Curaçaoan pianist and composer (Padu), born in Willemstad, Dutch Colony of Curaçao (d. 1998)
Truman Bradley, American actor (Science Fiction Theater), born in Sheldon, Missouri (d. 1974)
Artur Balsam, Polish-American classical pianist (Begeleidde Menuhin, Milstein), born in Warsaw, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1994)
Chester F. Carlson, American inventor (photocopying), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1968)
Henry Roth, American writer (Call it Sleep), born in Tysmenitz, Austria-Hungary (d. 1995)
Ray Middleton, American actor (1776; Hurricane Smith), born in Chicago Illinois (d. 1984)
Grigor Eghiazaryan, Armenian composer, born in Blur, Russian Empire (d. 1988)
Leslie Kenneth O'Brien, Lord O'Brien of Lothbury, English governor of the Bank of England (1966-73), born in London (d. 1995)
Myron McCormick, American actor (Hustler, Jolson Sings Again), born in Albany, Indiana (d. 1962)
Anne Hopkins Aitken, American Zen Buddhist and co-founder of Diamond Sangha, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1994)
Elizabeth Bishop, American poet (North & South, Pulitzer Prize 1956), born in Worcester, Massachusetts (d. 1979)
Henri Knap, Dutch journalist and writer (Per Ongeluk, De Weg naar Peruwelz), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1986)
William O. Darby, American Brigadier General and commander of Darby's Rangers in WWII, born in Fort Smith, Arkansas (d. 1945)[1]
Šimon Jurovský [Shimon Weiss-Nägel], Slovak composer for ballet, theater, and film, born in Uľanka, Banská Bystrica, Czechoslovakia (d. 1963)
John Grandy, British RAF-marshal, born in London, England (d. 2004)
Bert Haas, American baseball player, born in Naperville, Illinois (d. 1999)
Jules van Ackere, Flemish musicologist (Eternal Music; Maurice Ravel), born in Heule, Netherlands (d. 2008)
Georges Guetary, French singer and dancer (An American In Paris), born in Alexandria, Egypt (d. 1997)
Betty Field, American actress (Of Mice and Men, Kings Row, Bus Stop), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1973)
Max Rayne, English property developer and philanthropist (Rayne Foundation), born in London (d. 2003)
Buddy Morrow [Muni Zudekoff], American big band jazz and session trombonist and orchestra leader ("Night Train"), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 2010)

(Robert) "Buddy" Blattner, American table tennis player (World Championship - gold, doubles 1936, 1937), MLB baseball second baseman, 1942-49 (New York Giants, and two other teams), and broadcaster, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 2009)
Bruce Smith, American College Football Hall of Fame halfback (Heisman Trophy 1941, University of Minnesota; NFL: Green Bay Packers), born in Faribault, Minnesota (d. 1967)
Betsy Jochum, American baseball player and original member of the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 2025)[1]
Nexhmije Hoxha, Albanian wife of dictator Enver Hoxha 'Lady Macbeth of Albania', born in Manastir, Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (d. 2020)
Walter "Hoot" Evers, American baseball outfielder (MLB All-Star 1948, 50 Detroit Tigers), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1991)
Audrey Meadows [Cotter], American actress (The Honeymooners - "Alice Kramden"; The Jackie Gleason Show), born in New York City (d. 1996)
Manuel Oltra, Spanish secular and sacred music composer, and professor (Barcelona Conservatory, 1959-87), born in Valencia, Spain (d. 2015)
Yuri Averbach, Russian chess player (first centenarian FIDE Grandmaster), born in Kaluga, Russia (d. 2022)
Andrew MacElhone, British bartender, owner (Harry's New York Bar in Paris) and inventor of cocktails (Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails), born in Dundee, Scotland (d. 1996)
Joe Black, American Negro league and MLB player, born in Plainfield, New Jersey (d. 2002)
Lisel Mueller, German-American poet (Alive Together - Pulitzer Prize for Poetry 1997), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 2020)[1]
Alvin Brehm, American classical double bassist, conductor, and composer, born in New York City (d. 2014)
Raimondo d'Inzeo, Italian equestrian, 1st athlete to compete in 8 Olympic games (Olympic gold/2 silver/3 bronze-1948-76), born in Poggio Mirteto, Italy (d. 2013)
Neal Cassady, American counterculture figure, poet and writer of the Beat Generation (The First Third), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 1968)
John T. Myers, American politician (Rep-R-IN, 1967-97), born in Covington, Indiana (d. 2015)
Zdeněk Zouhar, Czech composer, and teacher, born in Kotvrdovice, Czechoslovakia (d. 2011)
Ada Lois Sipuel Fisher, American lawyer and civil rights activist (challenged University of Oklahoma segregation laws in Supreme Court and won), born in Chickasha, Oklahoma (d. 1995)
Jack Larson, American actor (The Adventures of Superman - "Jimmy Olsen"), screenwriter, and librettist (Virgil Thomson's "Lord Byron"), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2015)
Stanley Baker, Welsh actor and producer (Concrete Jungle, Zorro, Zulu), born in Ferndale, Glamorgan (d. 1976)
Roger Byrne, English soccer fullback (33 caps; Manchester United 245 games; died Munich air disaster), born in Manchester, Lancashire (d. 1958)
Alejandro Rey, Argentine-American actor and director (Carlos-Flying Nun), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1987)
Arlan Stangeland, American politician (Rep-R-Minnesota 1977-1991), born in North Dakota (d. 2013)
Catherine Hardy Lavender, American 4x100m runner (Olympic gold 1952), born in Carroll County, Georgia (d. 2017)
James Deetz, American anthropologist and historical archaeologist (In Small Things Forgotten: The Archaeology of Early American Life), born in Cumberland, Maryland (d. 2000)[1][2][3]
Manuel Castillo, Andalusian classical pianist and composer, born in Seville, Spain (d. 2005)
Larry Dolan, American baseball executive (principal owner Cleveland Guardians 2000-25) and media entrepreneur (founder FanDuel Sports Network Great Lakes), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2025)
Shadia [Fatmah Ahmed Kamal Shaker], Egyptian actress (Midaq Alley; The Thief and the Dogs), and singer, born in Sharqia Governorate, Egypt, birth date disputed (d. 2017)
Horst Eckel, German soccer wing half (32 caps West Germany, 1954 FIFA World Cup; 1. FC Kaiserslautern, SV Röchling Völklingen), born in Vogelbach, Germany (d. 2021)
Jan Christiaanse, Dutch university professor, and politician (Senate, 1973-77, 1979-91; resident of Dutch political party CDA), born in Gouda, Netherlands (d. 1991)
